lineage: Life and Works of Rufinus with Jerome's Apology Against Rufinus.
Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.

His confession of faith is unsatisfactory. No one asked him about the Trinity, but about Origen's doctrines of the Resurrection, the origin of souls, and the salvability of Satan. As to the Resurrection and to Satan he is ambiguous. As to souls he professes ignorance.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: “Still, since there may be some persons, who may wish to prove my faith, or to hear and learn what it is, I will declare that I thus think of the Trinity;”

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: At first you said that you entrusted your faith to the Bishop as a stick with which he might fortify himself on your behalf against those barking dogs.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Now you speak a little less confidently, “There may be some persons who wish to prove my faith.”

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: You begin to hesitate when the barkings which reach your ears are so numerous.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: I will not stop to discuss the forms of diction which you use, for these you look down upon and condemn: I will answer according to the meaning alone.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: You are asked about one thing, and you give account for yourself upon another.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: As to the doctrines of Arius, you contended against them at Alexandria a long time ago, by imprisonment and exile, not with words but with blood.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: But the question now relates to the heresy of Origen, and the feeling aroused against you on the subject.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: I should be sorry that you should trouble yourself to cure wounds which are already healed.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: You confess a Trinity in one Godhead.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: The whole world now confesses this, and I think that even the devils confess that the Son of God was born of the Virgin Mary, and took upon him the flesh and the soul belonging to human nature.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: But I must beg you not to think me a contentious man if I examine you a little more strictly.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: You say that the Son of God took the flesh and soul belonging to human nature.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Well then, I would ask you not to be vexed with me but to answer this question.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: That soul which Jesus took upon him, did it exist before it was born of Mary?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Was it created together with the body in that original Virgin nature which was begotten by the Holy Spirit? or, when the body was already formed within the womb, was it made all at once, and sent down from heaven?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: I wish to know which one of these you choose as your opinion.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: If it existed before it was born from Mary, then it was not yet the soul of Jesus; and it was employed in some way, and, for a reward of its virtues, it was made his soul.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: If it arose by traduction, then human souls, which we believe to be eternal, are subject to the same condition as those of the brutes, which perish with the body.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: But if it is created and sent into the body after the body has been formed, tell us so simply, and free us from anxiety.
